does: This paper introduces Imitation Learning from Language Feedback (ILF), a method for aligning language model outputs with human preferences using natural language critiques rather than binary comparisons. The approach iteratively conditions the model on an input, its initial output, and textual feedback to generate refinements, selects the refinement incorporating the most feedback, and finetunes the model to maximize likelihood of the chosen refinement. The authors establish a theoretical connection between ILF and Bayesian inference, relating it to reinforcement learning from human feedback. They validate ILF on controlled toy tasks and realistic summarization benchmarks, demonstrating that language feedback conveys richer preference information than pairwise comparisons alone.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in learning from human feedback for language model alignment, extending beyond the comparison-based approaches that dominated prior work. It responds to limitations of reward modeling and pairwise preference learning, which capture only coarse preference signals. The paper connects to broader literature on imitation learning and inverse reinforcement learning, reframing feedback incorporation as a form of Bayesian inference similar to RLHF but with richer signal. It also engages with concurrent work on using language as a medium for expressing preferences and critiques, positioning natural language feedback as more informative and interpretable than scalar rewards or binary judgments.
enables: ILF opens pathways toward more efficient alignment by leveraging the natural expressiveness of language feedback. This approach could enable scaling alignment to complex tasks where preference comparisons are ambiguous or where multiple valid solutions exist, since language can specify nuanced trade-offs. The framework suggests future work on automatically generating or synthesizing feedback at scale, studying how to handle contradictory or low-quality feedback, and extending the method beyond summarization to reasoning tasks, code generation, and interactive settings. The theoretical Bayesian connection could motivate new algorithmic improvements and convergence analysis, while the feedback generation component might enable weakly-supervised alignment without extensive human annotation.
